title: Purity, formulations and chromatography — identifying what's in a mixture
examTip: Rf = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent front. Always between 0 and 1.
introTips: A pure substance has a single, sharp melting point — a mixture melts over a range
Rf values let you identify unknown substances by comparing them to known references
Formulations are carefully designed mixtures — every ingredient has a specific job
intro: You've probably seen 'pure orange juice' on a carton, but in chemistry that juice is absolutely not pure — it's a mixture of water, sugars, acids and vitamins. Chemistry has its own strict meaning of 'pure', and it matters because purity affects how a substance behaves, how medicines work, and how we identify unknown compounds. This lesson covers three big ideas: what chemical purity actually means, how formulations are engineered to do a specific job, and how chromatography lets us tease apart the components of a mixture.

keyPoints.heading: Pure substances and how to test for purity
keyPoints.content: In chemistry, a pure substance contains only one type of element or compound — nothing else mixed in. The key practical test is the melting point (or boiling point): a pure substance melts at a single, sharp temperature, while a mixture melts over a range of temperatures. You compare your measured melting point to published data — if it matches exactly, the substance is likely pure. The everyday meaning of 'pure' (pure honey, pure orange juice) is completely different — those are still chemically impure mixtures. Knowing this distinction is crucial for exam questions that try to trick you.
writeBullets: Pure substance — contains only one element or compound, no mixing
Sharp melting point — melts at a single precise temperature (e.g. pure water: 100 °C)
Mixture — melts/boils over a range of temperatures, not a single point
Testing purity — compare measured melting/boiling point to data book values
Everyday 'pure' ≠ chemical 'pure' — orange juice is a mixture of many compounds
Impurities lower the melting point and widen the melting range

keyTerms.def: Contains only one type of element or compound — not mixed with anything else
The single temperature at which a pure substance changes from solid to liquid
Two or more substances combined but not chemically joined; melts over a range

keyPoints.memTip: Sharp = pure. Range = mixture. Simple as that.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse 'pure' in everyday language with the chemistry definition. Emphasise the melting point test as the reliable way to check.

keyPoints.heading: Formulations — designed mixtures with a purpose
keyPoints.content: A formulation is a mixture that has been carefully designed so that every component plays a specific role and together they give the product exactly the right properties. Formulations are made to precise specifications so they perform consistently every time. Medicines are classic formulations: the active ingredient does the therapeutic work, while binders, coatings and fillers control how the tablet holds together and how quickly it dissolves. Paints contain a pigment (colour), a binder (sticks to the surface), a solvent (controls viscosity) and additives (e.g. anti-fungal agents). Fuels, cleaning products, food products and alloys are all formulations. The key idea is that it's not random mixing — every ingredient is chosen for a reason.
writeBullets: Formulation — a carefully designed mixture where each component has a specific function
Medicine — active ingredient (effect) + binders + coatings + fillers
Paint — pigment (colour) + binder (adhesion) + solvent (viscosity) + additives
Fuel — blended hydrocarbons to give specific energy output and performance
Alloys — metals mixed to precise ratios for specific strength, corrosion resistance
Made to precise specifications — ensures consistent performance every time

keyTerms.def: A mixture deliberately designed so each component has a specific function
The component in a medicine that produces the therapeutic effect

keyPoints.memTip: Formulation = formula for a purpose. Every part earns its place.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Good real-world anchor: ask what each ingredient in bread does (flour = structure, yeast = CO₂ bubbles, water = hydration, salt = flavour + controls yeast).

keyPoints.heading: How chromatography works
keyPoints.content: Chromatography separates mixtures based on how strongly each component is attracted to the stationary phase compared to how well it dissolves in the mobile phase. In paper chromatography, the stationary phase is the paper (cellulose — polar), and the mobile phase is a solvent that moves up the paper by capillary action. Components that dissolve well in the solvent travel further up the paper; components more attracted to the paper move less far. Each substance has a characteristic Rf value in a given solvent, calculated as: Rf = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent front. Rf is always between 0 and 1 — it cannot be greater than 1 because the substance can never travel further than the solvent.
writeBullets: Stationary phase — doesn't move; paper chromatography uses cellulose paper
Mobile phase — moves through the stationary phase; e.g. a solvent travelling up paper
Capillary action — solvent rises up the paper without a pump
Rf value — distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent front
Rf always between 0 and 1 — substance cannot overtake the solvent front
High Rf — substance prefers mobile phase; low Rf — prefers stationary phase

keyTerms.def: The part of the chromatography system that does not move (e.g. paper)
The solvent that moves through the stationary phase, carrying the mixture
Retention factor = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent front
The ability of a liquid to rise up a narrow material without external force

keyPoints.memTip: Rf = substance ÷ solvent. Think 'how far did it get compared to the solvent?'
keyPoints.tutor_note: Get students to practise calculating Rf from diagrams. The most common error is measuring from the wrong baseline.

keyPoints.heading: Using chromatography to identify substances
keyPoints.content: To identify an unknown substance using paper chromatography, you run it alongside known reference substances on the same paper, using the same solvent. If the Rf values match, they are likely the same substance. A pure substance shows only one spot; a mixture shows multiple spots. Rf values can vary slightly with temperature, solvent purity and paper type, so running two different solvents gives more reliable identification. For more demanding analysis, gas chromatography (GC) vaporises the sample and carries it through a column with an inert carrier gas — substances separate by how strongly they interact with the column material and are identified by retention time. Linking GC to a mass spectrometer (GC-MS) gives highly accurate identification even of trace amounts.
writeBullets: Compare Rf values — unknown vs reference run on same paper in same solvent
Matching Rf values suggest the same substance
One spot = pure substance; multiple spots = mixture
Gas chromatography (GC) — vaporised sample separated in a column by inert carrier gas
Retention time — time taken for a substance to travel through the GC column; used to identify it
GC-MS — combines chromatography separation with mass spectrometry identification; very sensitive

keyTerms.def: A known compound run alongside an unknown to compare Rf values
Chromatography using a vaporised sample and an inert carrier gas through a column
The time taken for a substance to pass through a GC column; used to identify it
Gas chromatography linked to mass spectrometry — highly accurate identification of substances

keyPoints.memTip: One spot = pure. Many spots = mixture. GC adds heat and speed to paper chromatography.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often forget that Rf depends on the solvent — stress 'same solvent, same paper' when comparing.

tables.title: Paper chromatography vs gas chromatography
rows: Liquid or solid coating on column
Low — can see visible spots only

0.question: A sample melts between 118 °C and 124 °C. The data book value for this compound is 121 °C. What does this tell you?
options: The sample is impure — a melting range indicates it is a mixture
The sample is pure — 121 °C is within the range
The sample is pure — a melting range is normal for any substance
The sample is impure — it should melt below 100 °C
0.correctFeedback: Exactly right. A pure substance has a sharp melting point at a single temperature, not a range. A range means impurities are present.
0.wrongFeedback: A pure substance melts at one precise temperature. A melting range (not a single point) is the sign of a mixture or impure substance.

2.question: In a paper chromatography experiment, the solvent front travels 9.0 cm and a dye travels 6.3 cm. What is the Rf value of the dye?
2.correctFeedback: Correct! Rf = 6.3 ÷ 9.0 = 0.70. Always divide distance of substance by distance of solvent front.
2.wrongFeedback: Remember: Rf = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent front. So 6.3 ÷ 9.0 = 0.70. Never divide the other way — Rf can't be greater than 1.

3.question: A paper chromatography result shows three spots for a sample. What does this tell you?
options: The sample is a mixture containing at least three components
The sample is pure — spots are normal for pure substances
The solvent has reacted with the sample to make new products
The Rf values are all zero
3.correctFeedback: Spot on. Multiple spots = multiple components = a mixture. A pure substance would give exactly one spot.
3.wrongFeedback: Each spot represents one component in the mixture. One spot = pure substance. Three spots = mixture with at least three components.

commonMistakes: Confusing everyday 'pure' (e.g. pure orange juice) with the chemical definition — chemically pure means one substance only
Calculating Rf the wrong way up — always substance distance ÷ solvent distance, never the reverse
Thinking a melting range is normal — a pure substance has a sharp single melting point, not a range
Forgetting that Rf values depend on the solvent used — you can only compare Rf values run in the same solvent on the same type of paper
Confusing the stationary phase and mobile phase — mobile = moves (the solvent); stationary = stays still (the paper)
examTips: In Rf calculations, always measure from the same baseline (the pencil line where you spotted the sample) — not from the bottom of the paper
If asked to identify a substance, say 'the Rf value of the unknown matches that of compound X in the same solvent, so they may be the same substance' — don't just say 'it is X'
Gas chromatography and GC-MS appear in 6-mark questions about instrumental analysis — mention sensitivity, speed and ability to identify trace amounts
revisionCardBullets: Pure substance — one element or compound only; melts at a single sharp temperature
Mixture — melts over a range of temperatures (impurities lower and widen the melting range)
Everyday 'pure' ≠ chemical 'pure' — orange juice is chemically impure
Formulation — designed mixture where each component has a specific role (medicine, paint, fuel)
Paper chromatography — stationary phase = paper; mobile phase = solvent rising by capillary action
Rf = distance moved by substance ÷ distance moved by solvent front (always 0–1)
One spot = pure substance; multiple spots = mixture
Compare Rf of unknown to reference substances run in the same solvent to identify it
Gas chromatography (GC) — vaporised sample separated in a column; identified by retention time
GC-MS — GC linked to mass spectrometer; very sensitive, identifies trace amounts
Rf values vary with solvent and temperature — always run references alongside unknowns
Phytomining and bioleaching are mentioned in C10, not C8 — don't mix these up
